title: Walks
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $v,w \in G$.

  1. A walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a finite alternating sequence of adjacent nodes and edges \[v=v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n=w\]

    in $G$ for some $n \ge 0$, i.e.\ such that for every $k=0,\dots,n-1$ we have that $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \cup L$ or $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$, and with end nodes $v_0=v$ and $v_n=w$. An example walk from $v_0$ to $v_3$ could look like: \[v_0 \tuh v_1 \hut v_2 \huh v_3, \qquad\text{ with } \qquad v_0 \tuh v_1, v_2 \tuh v_1 \in E,\,v_2 \huh v_3 \in L. \] The same node may appear multiple times in a walk. Also the trivial walk consisting of a single node $v_0 \in G$ is allowed (if $v=w$). The walk is called into $v_0$ if $a_0 = v_0 \hus v_1$, and out of $v_0$ if $a_0 = v_0 \tuh v_1$. Similarly, it is called into $v_n$ if $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \suh v_n$ and out of $v_n$ if $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \hut v_n$.

  2. A directed walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form: \[v=v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\] for some $n \ge 0$, where all arrowheads point in the direction of $w$ and there are no arrowheads pointing back.
  3. A bidirected walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form: \[v=v_0 \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh v_n=w,\] for some $n \ge 0$, where all edges are bidirected.
  4. A collider walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form: \[v=v_0 \suh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus v_n=w,\] for some $n \ge 0$, where all nodes in between $v$ and $w$ have two arrowheads pointing towards them (a.k.a.\ collider). Note that for $n=1$ this reads: $v\sus w \in G$.
  5. A walk is called path if no node occurs more than once.
  6. A bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ is a walk of the form: \[v=v_0 \hut v_1 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \hus v_k \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\]

    such that $v \ne w$, the walk contains both endnodes exactly once, the subwalk $v_0 \hut \cdots v_{k-1}$ is a directed walk from $v_{k-1}$ to $v_0$, and the subwalk $v_k \tuh \cdots v_n$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_n$.

    If the edge $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$ is directed ($v_{k-1} \hut v_k$) then we say that the bifurcation has source $v_{k}$.

tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and the edge classifications of def def-edge-relations. Let $v, w \in J \cup V$.

  1. Walk. A walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is, for some integer $n \ge 0$, a tuple \[ (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] consisting of $n + 1$ vertices $v_0, v_1, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$ and $n$ ordered pairs $a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1} \in (J \cup V) \times (J \cup V)$, such that:
    1. $v_0 = v$ and $v_n = w$;
    2. for every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$, \[ \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr). \]

    The case $n = 0$ is admitted: the trivial walk is the single-vertex tuple $(v_0)$ with $v_0 = v = w$, no edges, and condition (b) vacuously satisfied; it is a walk only when $v = w$. For $n \ge 1$, vertices in the tuple may repeat, i.e.\ the same node may appear multiple times among $v_0, \dots, v_n$.

    End-node classifications. For a non-trivial walk ($n \ge 1$) we further classify behaviour at the two end-nodes:

    • the walk is into $v_0$ iff $a_0$ is an edge into $v_0$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.; concretely, iff $\bigl(a_0 = (v_1, v_0) \in E\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L\bigr)$;
    • the walk is out of $v_0$ iff $a_0$ is an edge out of $v_0$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item iii.; concretely, iff $a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in E$;
    • the walk is into $v_n$ iff $a_{n-1}$ is an edge into $v_n$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.; concretely, iff $\bigl(a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in E\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in L\bigr)$;
    • the walk is out of $v_n$ iff $a_{n-1}$ is an edge out of $v_n$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item iii.; concretely, iff $a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E$.

    On the trivial walk ($n = 0$) all four classifications are vacuously false (neither $a_0$ nor $a_{n-1}$ exists). On a non-trivial walk with a bidirected first edge ($a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L$) the walk is into $v_0$ but not out of $v_0$; hence “into $v_0$” and “out of $v_0$” are mutually exclusive but not jointly exhaustive over walks (and analogously at $v_n$).

  2. Directed walk. A directed walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ (for some $n \ge 0$) that, in addition, satisfies \[ a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \qquad \text{for every } k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}. \] The trivial walk ($n = 0$, $v = w$) is admitted as a directed walk from $v$ to itself.
  3. Bidirected walk. A bidirected walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ (for some $n \ge 0$) that, in addition, satisfies \[ a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L \qquad \text{for every } k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\} \] (equivalently, by the symmetry of $L$ from def def-cdmg, $(v_{k+1}, v_k) \in L$ for every such $k$). The trivial walk ($n = 0$, $v = w$) is admitted as a bidirected walk from $v$ to itself.
  4. Collider walk. A collider walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ (for some $n \ge 0$) satisfying the case-distinguished constraints below.

    • Case $n = 0$: no further constraint; the trivial walk is a collider walk from $v$ to itself when $v = w$.
    • Case $n = 1$: the single edge $a_0$ is required to be bidirected, namely \[ a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L \qquad \text{(equivalently, } (v, w) \in L\text{).} \] Purely directed edges $(v, w) \in E$ or $(w, v) \in E$ are not admitted as collider walks of length $1$.
    • Case $n \ge 2$: the edges satisfy: \begin{enumerate}[label=(*)]
    • (the first edge places an arrowhead at $v_1$) $\bigl(a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in E\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L\bigr)$;
    • (every interior edge is bidirected) for every $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, n - 2\}$, $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$;
    • (the last edge places an arrowhead at $v_{n-1}$) $\bigl(a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in L\bigr)$. \end{enumerate} Under (a)–(c), every interior node $v_k$ (i.e.\ $1 \le k \le n - 1$) has an arrowhead pointing toward it from each of its two incident walk-edges $a_{k-1}$ and $a_k$, recovering the verbal characterisation “every node strictly between $v$ and $w$ is a collider”.

    Reconciliation with the source block's $n = 1$ inline note. The source block contains the inline remark “Note that for $n = 1$ this reads: $v \sus w \in G$”. This remark is overridden by the strict reading of Case $n = 1$ above: for $n = 1$, a collider walk requires its unique edge to be bidirected, $a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L$. The relaxed reading “any $v \sus w$ edge” is not adopted here; in particular, neither $(v, w) \in E$ nor $(w, v) \in E$ qualifies as a collider walk of length $1$.

  5. Path. A walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is called a path iff its vertex sequence contains no repetitions, i.e.\ $v_i \ne v_j$ whenever $0 \le i < j \le n$. (Equivalently, the tuple $(v_0, v_1, \dots, v_n)$ consists of $n + 1$ pairwise distinct entries. The trivial walk $n = 0$ is vacuously a path.)
  6. Bifurcation. A bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ is a walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ for which there exists an index $k$ with $1 \le k \le n$ (in particular $n \ge 1$) such that all of the following hold:

    1. Distinct end-nodes appearing exactly once. $v \ne w$, and the end-nodes each occur exactly once in the vertex sequence: \[ v_0 \notin \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\} \qquad\text{and}\qquad v_n \notin \{v_0, v_1, \dots, v_{n-1}\}. \]
    2. Left subwalk is a directed walk from $v_{k-1}$ to $v_0$. For every $j \in \{0, 1, \dots, k - 2\}$, \[ a_j = (v_{j+1}, v_j) \in E. \] (When $k = 1$ the left subwalk is the trivial walk $(v_0)$ and this constraint is vacuous.)
    3. Right subwalk is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_n$. For every $j \in \{k, k + 1, \dots, n - 1\}$, \[ a_j = (v_j, v_{j+1}) \in E. \] (When $k = n$ the right subwalk is the trivial walk $(v_n)$ and this constraint is vacuous.)
    4. Middle (hinge) edge. The edge $a_{k-1}$ between $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ satisfies \[ \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L\bigr). \]
    5. (Addition: each end-node has exactly one arrowhead pointing toward it.) $a_0$ is an edge into $v_0$ and $a_{n-1}$ is an edge into $v_n$, in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.; equivalently, both end-nodes $v_0$ and $v_n$ have exactly one arrowhead pointing toward them (contributed by their unique incident walk-edges $a_0$ and $a_{n-1}$ respectively, uniqueness following from clause (a)).

      Spelled out as a case analysis on $k$:

      • if $k \ge 2$: clause (b) gives $a_0 = (v_1, v_0) \in E$, which is automatically an edge into $v_0$ (no further restriction);
      • if $k = 1$: $a_0 = a_{k-1}$ is the middle edge; both alternatives admitted by (d) — namely $a_0 = (v_1, v_0) \in E$ and $a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L$ — are edges into $v_0$, so no further restriction at $v_0$;
      • if $k \le n - 1$: clause (c) gives $a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in E$, which is automatically an edge into $v_n$ (no further restriction);
      • if $k = n$: $a_{n-1} = a_{k-1}$ is the middle edge; among the two alternatives admitted by (d), only the bidirected alternative $a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in L$ is an edge into $v_n$, whereas the directed alternative $a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E$ places its arrowhead at $v_{n-1}$ rather than $v_n$ and is therefore excluded.

      In particular, the degenerate configuration “$k = n$ together with the directed hinge $a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E$” — which would otherwise admit a tuple of the form $v_n \to v_{n-1} \to \cdots \to v_1 \to v_0$ (a plain directed walk from $v_n$ to $v_0$) as a “bifurcation” — is not a bifurcation under the present definition.

    Source of the bifurcation. If clause (d) is realised by the directed alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$, then we call $v_k$ the source of the bifurcation. If clause (d) is realised by the bidirected alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L$, no source is defined. Combined with clause (e), whenever a source $v_k$ is defined we automatically have $1 \le k \le n - 1$ (the case $k = n$ with directed hinge is excluded by (e)), so the source $v_k$ is an interior vertex of the walk and is distinct from both end-nodes $v_0 = v$ and $v_n = w$.

lean_blocks.code: variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]
lean_blocks.explanation: Section-wide `variable` declaration. Lean 4's `variable` mechanism makes `Node` and its `DecidableEq` instance available as auto-bound implicit arguments to every declaration below: any later `def`/`inductive` that mentions `Node` will silently pick up `{Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]` in its signature. `Type*` is the universe-polymorphic flavour (Lean 4 inserts a fresh universe variable), letting `Node` live in any universe. The `DecidableEq Node` typeclass is needed downstream to compute `Finset`-membership of edges in `CDMG`, to use `List.Nodup` in `IsPath`, and to call `List.tail` / `dropLast` / `getLast?` on vertex lists. This sets the type-level stage for the rest of the row.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Section-wide implicit binders, auto-added to every later declaration
-- `Type*` is universe-polymorphic; `DecidableEq` enables Finset/List ops
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code: variable {G : CDMG Node}
lean_blocks.explanation: Namespace-level `variable` declaration binding `G` implicitly. Every declaration inside the `Walk` namespace below (vertex/edge extractors, four end-node classifiers, walk-class predicates, bifurcation helpers) takes a walk of type `Walk G u v`, and Lean's auto-bound `variable` mechanism inserts `{G : CDMG Node}` into each of their signatures. Making `G` implicit lets downstream callers use dot-notation (`p.vertices` rather than `Walk.vertices G p`) since `G` is recoverable from the walk's type. The three-dash `-- def_3_4 --- start helper` marker used in the source file (visible in the surrounding comments) distinguishes this load-bearing infrastructure binder from the two-dash markers used on actual statement bodies.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Namespace-wide implicit `G`, inferred from `Walk G u v` at call sites
variable {G : CDMG Node}

lean_blocks.code: variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]
lean_blocks.explanation: Section-wide `variable` declaration. Lean 4's `variable` mechanism makes `Node` and its `DecidableEq` instance available as auto-bound implicit arguments to every declaration below: any later `def`/`inductive` that mentions `Node` will silently pick up `{Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]` in its signature. `Type*` is the universe-polymorphic flavour (Lean 4 inserts a fresh universe variable), letting `Node` live in any universe. The `DecidableEq Node` typeclass is needed downstream to compute `Finset`-membership of edges in `CDMG`, to use `List.Nodup` in `IsPath`, and to call `List.tail` / `dropLast` / `getLast?` on vertex lists. This sets the type-level stage for the rest of the row.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Section-wide implicit binders, auto-added to every later declaration
-- `Type*` is universe-polymorphic; `DecidableEq` enables Finset/List ops
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code: variable {G : CDMG Node}
lean_blocks.explanation: Namespace-level `variable` declaration binding `G` implicitly. Every declaration inside the `Walk` namespace below (vertex/edge extractors, four end-node classifiers, walk-class predicates, bifurcation helpers) takes a walk of type `Walk G u v`, and Lean's auto-bound `variable` mechanism inserts `{G : CDMG Node}` into each of their signatures. Making `G` implicit lets downstream callers use dot-notation (`p.vertices` rather than `Walk.vertices G p`) since `G` is recoverable from the walk's type. The three-dash `-- def_3_4 --- start helper` marker used in the source file (visible in the surrounding comments) distinguishes this load-bearing infrastructure binder from the two-dash markers used on actual statement bodies.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Namespace-wide implicit `G`, inferred from `Walk G u v` at call sites
variable {G : CDMG Node}

lean_blocks.code:
def vertices : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → List Node
  | _, _, .nil v _ => [v]
  | u, _, .cons _ _ p => u :: p.vertices
lean_blocks.explanation: `Walk.vertices p` extracts the LN's vertex tuple `[v_0, v_1, …, v_n]` as a `List Node` by structural recursion on the `Walk` constructors. The `nil v _` branch returns the singleton `[v]` — the trivial walk's only vertex. The `cons _ _ p` branch prepends the outer start vertex `u` (an explicit pattern parameter, recoverable from the cons-cell's outer index) to the recursive call on the tail `p`; this avoids double-counting the middle vertex `v`, which is already the head of `p.vertices`. Returning a `List` (not a `Finset`, not a length-indexed `Fin (n+1) → Node`) is the LN-faithful carrier: the LN's tuple is ordered and may repeat, and `List` unlocks Mathlib's `Nodup`/`head?`/`getLast?`/`tail`/`dropLast` API directly — each used by a downstream predicate in this file.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- The LN vertex tuple `[v_0, …, v_n]` as a `List Node`, by structural
-- recursion on the `Walk` constructors.
def vertices : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → List Node
  -- Trivial walk: the single vertex `v` IS the whole tuple.
  | _, _, .nil v _ => [v]
  -- Cons-cell: prepend the outer start `u`; the middle vertex `v` is
  -- already the head of `p.vertices`, so recursing on `p` avoids dup.
  | u, _, .cons _ _ p => u :: p.vertices

lean_blocks.code:
def IsColliderRest : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.nil _ _) => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.nil _ _) => True
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.nil _ _) => True
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.cons _ _ _) => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.cons _ _ _) => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (p@(.cons _ _ _)) => p.IsColliderRest
lean_blocks.explanation: `IsColliderRest` carries the trailing constraint of an $n \ge 2$ collider walk after its first edge has been consumed: every interior `WalkStep` must be bidirected, and the last `WalkStep` must place an arrowhead at $v_{n-1}$. The recursion reads the cons-cell's typed `WalkStep` directly. When the tail is `.nil _ _` (the outer cons IS the last edge): `.forwardE _` is rejected (forward `E` places no arrowhead at the current start `u = v_{n-1}`), `.backwardE _` is admitted (encodes `(v, u) ∈ G.E`, arrowhead at `u`), `.bidir _` is admitted (arrowheads at both endpoints). When the tail is `.cons …` (a true interior edge): only `.bidir _` is admitted, and the deeper tail recursively satisfies the same predicate. The `nil` outer-walk branch returns `True` for totality; it is unreachable from the only call site (the $n \ge 2$ branches of `IsColliderWalk`).
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Trailing constraint for an n >= 2 collider walk after the first edge
-- has been consumed: interior edges bidirected, last edge arrowheads at u.
def IsColliderRest : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- Unreachable from the call site; True for totality.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  -- Last edge is forward E: no arrowhead at the current start u; rejected.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.nil _ _) => False
  -- Last edge is backward E: (v, u) ∈ E places arrowhead at u; admitted.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.nil _ _) => True
  -- Last edge is bidirected: arrowhead at both endpoints; admitted.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.nil _ _) => True
  -- Interior edge: must be bidirected (LN clause iv(b)).
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.cons _ _ _) => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.cons _ _ _) => False
  -- Bidirected interior edge; recurse on the deeper tail.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (p@(.cons _ _ _)) => p.IsColliderRest

lean_blocks.code:
def IsBifurcationWithSplit : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.nil _ _), 0 => True
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.cons _ _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (p@(.cons _ _ _)), 0 => p.IsDirectedWalk
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (p@(.cons _ _ _)), 0 => p.IsDirectedWalk
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) _, _ + 1 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) p, k + 1 => p.IsBifurcationWithSplit k
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) _, _ + 1 => False
lean_blocks.explanation: `IsBifurcationWithSplit p k` says: `p` is a bifurcation in which the left arm has exactly `k` reverse-directed edges, so the hinge edge sits at edge position `k` (the LN's split index is $k + 1$). Reads the cons-cell's typed `WalkStep` directly under three regimes. At $k = 0$ with `.nil`-tail (the hinge is also the last edge, LN's $n = 1$ case), only `.bidir _` is admitted: per the operator addition, a directed hinge at $n = 1$ would leave $v_n$ with no arrowhead. At $k = 0$ with `.cons`-tail (right arm non-trivial), both `.backwardE _` and `.bidir _` are admissible hinges (both place an arrowhead at $v_{k-1}$), and the right arm `p` must satisfy `IsDirectedWalk`; `.forwardE _` is rejected. At $k + 1$ (still in the left arm), only `.backwardE _` is admitted — every left-arm edge is an `E`-edge running backward toward $v_0$ — and the recursion descends with `k` decremented. The `.nil` outer-walk branch returns `False` (a bifurcation has at least the hinge edge).
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- p is a bifurcation with left arm of length k: hinge at edge position k,
-- LN's split index is k + 1.
def IsBifurcationWithSplit : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ → Prop
  -- Trivial walk has no hinge; no split works.
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  -- n = 1, k = 0: hinge is the last edge. Only bidirected admitted —
  -- directed hinges would leave v_n with no inbound arrowhead.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.nil _ _), 0 => True
  -- n ≥ 2, k = 0: forward-E first edge cannot be the hinge (wrong arrow).
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.cons _ _ _), 0 => False
  -- Directed hinge; right arm must be a directed walk (LN clause vi(c)).
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (p@(.cons _ _ _)), 0 => p.IsDirectedWalk
  -- Bidirected hinge; right arm must be a directed walk.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (p@(.cons _ _ _)), 0 => p.IsDirectedWalk
  -- k + 1: still in the left arm. Only backward-E admitted (LN clause vi(b)).
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) _, _ + 1 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) p, k + 1 => p.IsBifurcationWithSplit k
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) _, _ + 1 => False

lean_blocks.code:
def IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit :
    ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.cons _ _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (p@(.cons _ _ _)), 0 => p.IsDirectedWalk
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.cons _ _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) _, _ + 1 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) p, k + 1 =>
      p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit k
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) _, _ + 1 => False
lean_blocks.explanation: Variant of `IsBifurcationWithSplit` restricted to a directed hinge — the LN clause vi(d) first alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$. This is the precondition for the LN's "source $v_k$" to be defined: only directed hinges have a source. All length-1 walks (`.nil`-tail at $k = 0$, all three constructor tags) return `False`: per the operator addition, a directed hinge at $n = 1$ leaves $v_n$ with no inbound arrowhead, and the bidirected case is the alternative this helper specifically excludes. At $k = 0$ with non-trivial right arm, only `.backwardE _` is admitted (with `p.IsDirectedWalk` pinning the right arm); `.bidir _` is rejected by construction. At $k + 1$ the left-arm constraint is identical to the general helper: only `.backwardE _`, recurse with `k` decremented. Combined with the addition, whenever this predicate holds for some split index `i`, the LN's index satisfies $1 \le k \le n - 1$, so the source $v_k = v_{i+1}$ is automatically interior.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Directed-hinge variant of `IsBifurcationWithSplit`: clause vi(d) first
-- alternative only; precondition for the LN's "source v_k" to be defined.
def IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit :
    ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  -- n = 1 with directed hinge: v_n has no arrowhead. Bidirected: not this variant.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  -- n ≥ 2, k = 0: only backward-E hinge admitted; right arm must be directed.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.cons _ _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (p@(.cons _ _ _)), 0 => p.IsDirectedWalk
  -- Bidirected hinge excluded by construction (this is the directed variant).
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.cons _ _ _), 0 => False
  -- k + 1: left-arm step must be backward-E; recurse.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) _, _ + 1 => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) p, k + 1 =>
      p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit k
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) _, _ + 1 => False

lean_blocks.code:
inductive WalkStep (G : CDMG Node) : Node → Node → Type _ where
  | forwardE  {u v : Node} (h : (u, v) ∈ G.E) : WalkStep G u v
  | backwardE {u v : Node} (h : (v, u) ∈ G.E) : WalkStep G u v
  | bidir     {u v : Node} (h : s(u, v) ∈ G.L) : WalkStep G u v
lean_blocks.explanation: `WalkStep G u v` is the typed carrier of "a valid walk-edge from `u` to `v` in `G`": a `Type _`-level inductive whose three constructors enumerate the three LN-admissible channel/direction combinations a walk-edge can take. `.forwardE` carries `(u, v) ∈ G.E` (LN's `\tuh`, arrowhead at `v`); `.backwardE` carries `(v, u) ∈ G.E` (LN's `\hut`, arrowhead at `u`); `.bidir` carries `s(u, v) ∈ G.L` where `s(·,·)` is Mathlib's `Sym2` constructor, encoding the LN's `\huh` (arrowheads at both endpoints). Only three constructors, not four: a putative `.backwardL` is byte-identical to `.bidir` because `G.L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` is an unordered-pair carrier with `s(u, v) = s(v, u)` definitionally. The endpoints `u v` are implicit because they are determined by the surrounding `Walk` type indices at every construction site.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Typed walk-step from u to v: three constructors enumerate the three
-- LN-admissible channel/direction combinations.
inductive WalkStep (G : CDMG Node) : Node → Node → Type _ where
  -- Forward E-edge: (u, v) ∈ G.E. LN's \tuh, arrowhead at v.
  | forwardE  {u v : Node} (h : (u, v) ∈ G.E) : WalkStep G u v
  -- Backward E-edge: (v, u) ∈ G.E. LN's \hut, arrowhead at u.
  | backwardE {u v : Node} (h : (v, u) ∈ G.E) : WalkStep G u v
  -- Bidirected L-edge: s(u, v) ∈ G.L (Sym2 unordered pair). LN's \huh.
  | bidir     {u v : Node} (h : s(u, v) ∈ G.L) : WalkStep G u v

lean_blocks.code:
inductive Walk (G : CDMG Node) : Node → Node → Type _ where
  | nil  (v : Node) (hv : v ∈ G) : Walk G v v
  | cons {u w : Node} (v : Node)
      (s : WalkStep G u v) (p : Walk G v w) : Walk G u w
lean_blocks.explanation: `Walk G u v` is the inductive type of finite alternating sequences of vertices and edges from `u` to `v` in `G`, mirroring Mathlib's `SimpleGraph.Walk` and adapted to CDMGs by widening the per-edge constraint to the typed `WalkStep`. `Walk.nil v hv` is the trivial walk on a single vertex `v ∈ G` — the type index `Walk G v v` enforces the LN's `v = w` precondition at the type level, and the membership witness `hv` is data on the constructor because for $n = 0$ no edge from which to recover the `J ∪ V` membership exists. `Walk.cons v s p` prepends the alternating step `v_0, a_0, v_1` to an existing walk `p : Walk G v w`: the middle vertex `v` is an explicit parameter (so downstream pattern matches reach it without `(v := …)` syntax), `s : WalkStep G u v` is the typed step witness, and the outer endpoints `u, w` are implicit (pinned by the outer walk's type indices). No stored ordered pair on `cons`: the channel and pair are recovered from `s`'s constructor tag, eliminating channel confusion under walk reversal.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- A walk from u to v: alternating sequence of vertices and typed steps.
inductive Walk (G : CDMG Node) : Node → Node → Type _ where
  -- Trivial walk: single vertex v ∈ G; type index pins endpoints to v = v.
  -- The membership witness hv is stored because no edge can recover it.
  | nil  (v : Node) (hv : v ∈ G) : Walk G v v
  -- Cons-cell: prepend the step `u → v` to a walk `p : Walk G v w`.
  -- Middle vertex v explicit; outer endpoints u, w implicit (inferred).
  | cons {u w : Node} (v : Node)
      (s : WalkStep G u v) (p : Walk G v w) : Walk G u w

lean_blocks.code:
def intoStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) _ => True
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) _ => True
lean_blocks.explanation: `p.intoStart` iff `p` is non-trivial AND its first `WalkStep` places an arrowhead at $v_0$ (the LN's `\hus` at the start node). Case-splits on the cons-cell's typed `WalkStep` constructor: `.forwardE` encodes `(v_0, v_1) ∈ E` with the arrowhead at $v_1$ (LN's `\tuh`), so it is not into $v_0$ — `False`; `.backwardE` encodes `(v_1, v_0) ∈ E` with the arrowhead at $v_0$, qualifying as into $v_0$ — `True`; `.bidir` encodes `s(v_0, v_1) ∈ L`, with an arrowhead at both endpoints (including $v_0$) — `True`. The trivial walk is vacuously `False`: no `a_0` exists, so the LN's existentially-loaded "$a_0 = \ldots$" clause is false. A bidirected first edge produces `intoStart ∧ ¬outOfStart` — the LN's classifiers are mutually exclusive but not jointly exhaustive.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Non-trivial walk whose first WalkStep places an arrowhead at v_0
-- (LN's \hus). Reads the cons-cell's WalkStep constructor tag directly.
def intoStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- Trivial walk: no first edge exists; vacuously false.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  -- Forward E: arrowhead at v_1, tail at v_0; not into v_0.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) _ => False
  -- Backward E: (v_1, v_0) ∈ E places arrowhead at v_0.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) _ => True
  -- Bidirected: arrowhead at both endpoints, including v_0.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) _ => True

lean_blocks.code:
def outOfStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) _ => True
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) _ => False
lean_blocks.explanation: `p.outOfStart` iff `p` is non-trivial AND its first `WalkStep` is a forward-`E` edge `(v_0, v_1) ∈ E` (the LN's `\tuh` — tail at $v_0$, arrowhead at $v_1$). Case-splits on the cons-cell's typed `WalkStep`: only `.forwardE` qualifies — `True`; `.backwardE` puts an arrowhead at $v_0$, not a tail — `False`; `.bidir` is `\huh`, with arrowheads at both endpoints — also `False`, because the LN's "out of" relation (`def_3_3` item iii) excludes `L`-edges entirely. The trivial walk is vacuously `False`. Mirror of `intoStart` with two structural twists: only `.forwardE` survives (versus `intoStart`'s `.backwardE`+`.bidir`), and a bidirected first edge produces the non-partition shape `intoStart ∧ ¬outOfStart`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Non-trivial walk whose first WalkStep is a forward-E edge — LN's \tuh
-- at v_0 (tail at v_0, arrowhead at v_1). E-only; bidirected excluded.
def outOfStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- Trivial walk: vacuously false.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  -- Forward E: (v_0, v_1) ∈ E, tail at v_0; the only admissible case.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) _ => True
  -- Backward E: arrowhead at v_0; not out of v_0.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) _ => False
  -- Bidirected: arrowhead at v_0; LN's `outOf` excludes L-edges entirely.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) _ => False

lean_blocks.code:
def intoEnd : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.nil _ _) => True
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.nil _ _) => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.nil _ _) => True
  | _, _, .cons _ _ (p@(.cons _ _ _)) => p.intoEnd
lean_blocks.explanation: `p.intoEnd` iff `p` is non-trivial AND its last `WalkStep` places an arrowhead at $v_n$ (the LN's `\suh` at the end node). Recurses on `Walk` directly: peel cons-cells until the tail is `.nil _ _`, at which point the outer cons-cell's `WalkStep` is the last edge. Case-splits on its constructor: `.forwardE` encodes `(v_{n-1}, v_n) ∈ E` with the arrowhead at $v_n$ — `True`; `.backwardE` encodes `(v_n, v_{n-1}) ∈ E` with the tail at $v_n$ — `False`; `.bidir` encodes `s(v_{n-1}, v_n) ∈ L` with an arrowhead at $v_n$ — `True`. The recursive case `cons _ _ (p@(.cons …))` ignores the outer step (only the last step matters) and recurses on the strictly-smaller tail. The trivial walk is vacuously `False`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Non-trivial walk whose last WalkStep places an arrowhead at v_n
-- (LN's \suh). Peels cons-cells until the tail is nil, then case-splits.
def intoEnd : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- Trivial walk: vacuously false.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  -- Last edge forward E: arrowhead at v_n.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.nil _ _) => True
  -- Last edge backward E: tail at v_n; not into v_n.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.nil _ _) => False
  -- Last edge bidirected: arrowhead at both endpoints.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.nil _ _) => True
  -- Not yet at the last edge: ignore the outer step, recurse on the tail.
  | _, _, .cons _ _ (p@(.cons _ _ _)) => p.intoEnd

lean_blocks.code:
def outOfEnd : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.nil _ _) => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.nil _ _) => True
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.nil _ _) => False
  | _, _, .cons _ _ (p@(.cons _ _ _)) => p.outOfEnd
lean_blocks.explanation: `p.outOfEnd` iff `p` is non-trivial AND its last `WalkStep` is a backward-`E` edge `(v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E$ (the LN's `\hut` at the end node — tail at $v_n$, arrowhead at $v_{n-1}$). Same direct-recursion access path as `intoEnd`: peel cons-cells until the tail is `.nil _ _`, then case-split on the last cons-cell's `WalkStep`. `.forwardE` places an arrowhead at $v_n$ — `False`; `.backwardE` places a tail at $v_n$ — `True`; `.bidir` places an arrowhead at $v_n$ — `False`, because the LN's "out of" relation excludes `L`-edges. Mirror of `intoEnd` with the `True`/`False` assignments swapped on the `E`-channel and the bidirected case rejected. Trivial walk vacuously `False`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Non-trivial walk whose last WalkStep is a backward-E edge — LN's \hut
-- at v_n (tail at v_n, arrowhead at v_{n-1}). E-only; bidirected excluded.
def outOfEnd : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- Trivial walk: vacuously false.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  -- Last edge forward E: arrowhead at v_n; not out of v_n.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.nil _ _) => False
  -- Last edge backward E: (v_n, v_{n-1}) ∈ E, tail at v_n.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.nil _ _) => True
  -- Last edge bidirected: arrowhead at v_n; L excluded from `outOf`.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.nil _ _) => False
  -- Recurse on the tail until the last cons-cell is reached.
  | _, _, .cons _ _ (p@(.cons _ _ _)) => p.outOfEnd

lean_blocks.code:
def IsDirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) p => p.IsDirectedWalk
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) _ => False
lean_blocks.explanation: `p.IsDirectedWalk` iff every `WalkStep` of `p` is a `.forwardE _` — the LN clause ii requirement that every edge satisfies $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$. Reads the cons-cell's typed `WalkStep` directly: `.forwardE _` advances the recursion on the tail; `.backwardE _` is rejected because it encodes the reverse writing `(v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E`, not the LN's forward writing; `.bidir _` is rejected because it encodes an `L`-channel edge, not an `E`-channel edge. The trivial walk satisfies vacuously (`True`), matching the LN's explicit "the trivial walk is admitted as a directed walk". A direct constructor case-split is preferred over an existential `∃ h, s = .forwardE h ∧ …` because the unfolding is flat: `simp` reduces `IsDirectedWalk` on a known cons-cell in one step with no existential to discharge.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Every WalkStep is a forward-E step (LN clause ii: every edge is
-- (v_k, v_{k+1}) ∈ E). Trivial walk vacuously admitted.
def IsDirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- Trivial walk: vacuously a directed walk.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  -- Forward E step: advance the recursion on the tail.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) p => p.IsDirectedWalk
  -- Backward E: reverse writing, LN-rejected.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) _ => False
  -- Bidirected: L-channel, not E-channel; LN-rejected.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) _ => False

lean_blocks.code:
def IsBidirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) p => p.IsBidirectedWalk
lean_blocks.explanation: `p.IsBidirectedWalk` iff every `WalkStep` of `p` is a `.bidir _` — the LN clause iii requirement that every edge satisfies $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$. Mirror of `IsDirectedWalk` with the channel selector swapped: `.bidir _` (encoding `s(u, v) \in G.L`) advances the recursion on the tail; both `E`-channel tags `.forwardE _` and `.backwardE _` are rejected. The trivial walk satisfies vacuously. The LN's parenthetical "equivalently, by the symmetry of $L$ … $(v_{k+1}, v_k) \in L$" is automatic here: `G.L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` uses unordered pairs, so `.bidir` is channel-symmetric by construction and no `hL_symm` invocation is needed to handle the reverse writing.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Every WalkStep is a bidirected step (LN clause iii: every edge is
-- (v_k, v_{k+1}) ∈ L). Sym2 symmetry makes direction automatic.
def IsBidirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- Trivial walk: vacuously bidirected.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  -- Forward E: E-channel, not L; rejected.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) _ => False
  -- Backward E: also E-channel; rejected.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) _ => False
  -- Bidirected L step: advance the recursion on the tail.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) p => p.IsBidirectedWalk

lean_blocks.code:
def IsColliderWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.nil _ _) => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.nil _ _) => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.nil _ _) => True
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (p@(.cons _ _ _)) => p.IsColliderRest
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.cons _ _ _) => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (p@(.cons _ _ _)) => p.IsColliderRest
lean_blocks.explanation: `p.IsColliderWalk` encodes the three-case LN spec for clause iv. Case $n = 0$ (trivial walk): no constraint — `True`. Case $n = 1$ (cons-cell with `.nil`-tail): only `.bidir _` is admitted — `True`; both `.forwardE _` and `.backwardE _` are `False`. This is the load-bearing override `[collider_walk_n1_form_contradicts_inline_note]`: the LN's symbolic pattern collapses for $n = 1$ to require arrowheads at both endpoints, forcing the lone edge into `L`; purely directed edges are not admitted as collider walks of length 1. Case $n \ge 2$ (cons-cell with `.cons`-tail): the first step must place an arrowhead at $v_1$, so `.forwardE _` and `.bidir _` are admitted (both with the trailing constraint `p.IsColliderRest`), and `.backwardE _` is rejected (it would put the arrowhead at $v_0$, not $v_1$). `IsColliderRest` carries the LN's interior-bidirected + last-edge-arrowhead-at-$v_{n-1}$ constraint.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Collider walk: case-split on the LN's n = 0 / n = 1 / n ≥ 2 regimes.
def IsColliderWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- n = 0: trivial walk admitted vacuously.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  -- n = 1: strict bidirected-only reading (operator addition override).
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (.nil _ _) => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.nil _ _) => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (.nil _ _) => True
  -- n ≥ 2: first edge places arrowhead at v_1 (forward-E or bidir);
  -- the rest of the walk satisfies `IsColliderRest`.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.forwardE _) (p@(.cons _ _ _)) => p.IsColliderRest
  -- Backward E: arrowhead at v_0, not v_1; rejected.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.backwardE _) (.cons _ _ _) => False
  -- Bidir first edge: arrowhead at both endpoints, including v_1.
  | _, _, .cons _ (.bidir _) (p@(.cons _ _ _)) => p.IsColliderRest

lean_blocks.code: def IsPath {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop := p.vertices.Nodup
lean_blocks.explanation: One-liner predicate: `p.IsPath` iff the vertex list `p.vertices` is duplicate-free, encoded via mathlib's `List.Nodup`. The LN's "no node occurs more than once" (item v) is exactly this. The trivial walk's singleton vertex list `[v_0]` is vacuously `Nodup` (via `List.nodup_singleton`), so the trivial walk is a path — matching the LN rewrite's "vacuously a path" reading. Defining `IsPath` over `List.Nodup` (rather than as a fresh structural recursion on `Walk`) inherits decidability, monotonicity, and every other mathlib `Nodup` lemma for free. Index-based pairwise-distinctness (`∀ i < j, vᵢ ≠ vⱼ`) is the equivalent classical form but is non-idiomatic in mathlib.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- A path is a walk whose vertex list has no repeats (mathlib `List.Nodup`)
def IsPath {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop := p.vertices.Nodup

lean_blocks.code:
def IsBifurcation {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  u ≠ v ∧
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationWithSplit i
lean_blocks.explanation: Main bifurcation predicate (LN item vi), built as a four-way conjunction: (i) `u ≠ v` — distinct end-nodes (LN clause (a) first half); (ii) `u ∉ p.vertices.tail` — `u` does not reappear after position 0 (LN's `v_0 ∉ {v_1, …, v_n}`); (iii) `v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast` — `v` does not appear before the last position (LN's `v_n ∉ {v_0, …, v_{n-1}}`); (iv) `∃ i, p.IsBifurcationWithSplit i` — there exists a split index packaging LN clauses (b)–(d) plus the operator addition (e). This is weaker than `IsPath`'s `Nodup`: interior vertices may repeat. `List.tail` and `List.dropLast` are Mathlib convenience functions for "drop first/last element".
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- A bifurcation between `u` and `v`: distinct endpoints + a valid split
def IsBifurcation {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  -- LN clause (a): endpoints differ
  u ≠ v ∧
  -- u appears exactly once (only at position 0); `tail` drops the head
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  -- v appears exactly once (only at last position); `dropLast` drops the tail
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  -- LN clauses (b)–(d) plus addition (e), packaged via the existential split
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationWithSplit i

lean_blocks.code:
def IsBifurcationSource {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v)
    (x : Node) : Prop :=
  u ≠ v ∧
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit i ∧
       p.vertices[i + 1]? = some x
lean_blocks.explanation: `p.IsBifurcationSource x` iff `p` is a bifurcation between `u` and `v` AND there is a split index `i` for which the hinge is directed AND `x = v_{i+1}` (the LN's `v_k` for `k = i + 1`). Repeats `IsBifurcation`'s three endnode-uniqueness clauses (`u ≠ v`, `u ∉ p.vertices.tail`, `v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast`) so the predicate stands on its own, then existentially quantifies over the directed-hinge split helper: `∃ i, p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit i ∧ p.vertices[i + 1]? = some x`. The `[i + 1]?` accessor returns `Option Node`, unifying with `some x` without threading an in-bounds proof. Combined with the operator addition (enforced inside `IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit`'s length-1 branches), whenever the predicate holds the split satisfies $1 \le k \le n - 1$, so the source `x` is automatically an interior vertex distinct from both endpoints.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- x is a source of bifurcation p: directed-hinge bifurcation at index i
-- with x at vertex position i + 1 (= LN's v_k for k = i + 1).
def IsBifurcationSource {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v)
    (x : Node) : Prop :=
  -- Endnodes distinct (LN clause (a) first half).
  u ≠ v ∧
  -- u appears only at position 0 (LN: v_0 ∉ {v_1, …, v_n}).
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  -- v appears only at the last position (LN: v_n ∉ {v_0, …, v_{n-1}}).
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  -- A directed-hinge split exists at index i, with x at position i + 1.
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit i ∧
       p.vertices[i + 1]? = some x

addition_to_the_LN:
[bifurcation_right_chain_trivial_is_just_directed_walk] As an additional constraint on the bifurcation form, both endnodes $v_0$ and $v_n$ must have exactly one arrowhead pointing towards them. In particular, this excludes the degenerate case $k = n$ with directed hinge $v_{n-1} \hut v_n$, in which the walk reduces to a directed walk $v_0 \leftarrow v_1 \leftarrow \cdots \leftarrow v_n$ and $v_n$ has no arrowhead pointing towards it.

[collider_walk_n1_form_contradicts_inline_note] For $n=1$, a collider walk from $v$ to $w$ requires a bidirected edge $v \huh w \in E$ (arrowheads at both endpoints), as dictated by the form $v_0 \suh v_1$ combined with $v_{n-1} \hus v_n$ collapsing onto the single edge. The inline note "$v \sus w \in G$" is to be read in this stricter sense and does *not* admit purely directed edges $v \tuh w$ or $v \hut w$ as collider walks of length 1.
design_choices:
**A walk is an `inductive Type` indexed by both endpoints, mirroring Mathlib's `SimpleGraph.Walk`.** A flat record bundling a list of edges with a separate coherence proof is rejected because downstream chapters pattern-match on walks at every recursion step; a list-plus-witness pair would force each consumer to destructure the coherence proof one edge at a time. An un-indexed `Walk G` with endpoints in fields would force endpoint equalities through every "walks from $u$ to $v$" quantifier. The middle vertex on `cons` is made explicit (departing from `SimpleGraph.Walk` here) so downstream pattern matches reach it without `(v := …)` named-pattern syntax.

**The per-step constraint is a typed `Type _`-level inductive `WalkStep G u v` with three constructors `.forwardE`, `.backwardE`, `.bidir`, not a `Prop`-level disjunction over a stored ordered pair.** A `Prop`-level disjunction would force `Walk.cons` to carry both the ordered pair `a : Node × Node` and a separate witness, exposing a two-way `Or` at every cons-cell destructure and doubling the case count of every downstream recursion. Promoting the constraint to a `Type _` inductive aligns it with the `Walk` carrier itself: the channel (`E` vs `L`) and direction (forward vs backward `E`) become constructor tags, the endpoints become type indices, and the membership witness becomes a single constructor argument. Channel preservation under walk reversal is automatic — `.bidir h` stays `.bidir h` — eliminating the need to invoke `hL_symm` on every reversal and ruling out silent misclassification of an `L`-step as an `E`-step on the writing-mirror class of CDMGs.

**Only three constructors on `WalkStep`, not four.** A fourth `.backwardL` is collapsed into `.bidir` because `G.L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` is an unordered-pair carrier where `s(u, v) = s(v, u)` holds definitionally via Mathlib's `Sym2` swap quotient; the bidirected channel is direction-symmetric by construction. The `E`-channel still carries direction (`(u, v) ∈ G.E` and `(v, u) ∈ G.E` are independent ordered-pair memberships), so the `forwardE`/`backwardE` split is preserved. The asymmetry between the two channels is a direct consequence of `def_3_1`'s carrier choice.

**The five derived walk-flavours are `Prop`-predicates on the single `Walk` type, not separate inductives.** Treating each as its own `inductive DirectedWalk` would duplicate the structural recursion and force a forgetful map plus lift-up lemmas in every downstream row. A `Subtype` is dispreferred since it changes walk equality, and a bundled `Bifurcation` `structure` is rejected on similar grounds — the source phrases these as yes/no classifications of a walk, and a `(p, hp)` pair carries exactly that information. Crucially, "every directed walk is a walk" becomes the identity function.

**The four end-node classifiers are four independent predicates, not a partition-style enum or a `Bool × Bool` flag.** They are deliberately not jointly exhaustive: the trivial walk satisfies none of them, and a bidirected first edge yields `intoStart ∧ ¬outOfStart`. An enum would need a fifth `none` constructor; a bit-pair would compress two distinct symbols ($\hus$ vs $\tuh$) into bit positions and lose the LN-macro-grep correspondence. Each predicate consumes the cons-cell's `WalkStep` constructor tag directly — `.forwardE` / `.backwardE` / `.bidir` map onto the LN's symbolic readings one-for-one.

**`intoEnd` and `outOfEnd` recurse on `Walk` directly to reach the last cons-cell, instead of going through an edges-list accessor.** No `Walk.edges` projection exists: the cons-cell stores no ordered pair, and the `.bidir` constructor's `Sym2` payload has no canonical ordered-pair representative. A sigma-typed `lastStep` helper is rejected because it would force a wrap/unwrap pass plus a correctness lemma; the duplicated "peel cons-cells until the tail is `.nil`" recursion across the two predicates is shallow, self-contained, and keeps the LN correspondence readable in a single-file local view.

**Two auxiliary predicates `IsColliderRest` and `IsBifurcationWithSplit` encode the positional case-splits.** An inline single recursion would need boolean flags or length comparisons per cons-cell to distinguish first / interior / last edge for the collider walk, and would need to thread `k ≤ length p` everywhere for the bifurcation. Factoring "interior + last" into `IsColliderRest` lets `IsColliderWalk` handle only the first edge. Quantifying over an integer split index $k$ and indexing into an edge list is rejected for the bifurcation; the helper's `ℕ`-counter is decremented by structural recursion and the `.nil` branch returns `False`, eliminating bound plumbing.

**A separate `IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit` helper, rather than a `Bool` flag on the general bifurcation predicate.** The two predicates differ on the $n = 1$ case — the general form admits the bidirected hinge, the directed-hinge variant excludes every length-1 walk per the operator addition. A shared predicate with a flag would conflate these. `IsBifurcationSource` is then a `Prop`-predicate on a candidate vertex rather than an `Option Node` accessor, avoiding sentinel values; the source position is read via `p.vertices[i + 1]?` rather than `List.get` with a threaded in-bounds proof.

**The vertex sequence is a `List Node`, not a `Finset` nor a length-indexed `Fin (n+1) → Node`.** A `Finset` is wrong on both counts (the tuple is ordered and may repeat); the indexed function would force `Fin`-arithmetic plumbing at every use site, whereas `List` unlocks Mathlib's `Nodup`, `head?`, `getLast?`, `tail`, and `dropLast` API directly — and makes `IsPath` a one-liner over `List.Nodup`.

**Trivial walks satisfy the walk-class predicates vacuously (`True`), but the four end-node classifiers vacuously `False`.** The asymmetry is intentional: directed / bidirected / collider / path explicitly admit the trivial walk, but the classifiers are existentially loaded on $a_0$ or $a_{n-1}$ existing, so `False` is the faithful encoding. The convention is load-bearing for `def_3_6` acyclicity, which counts non-trivial directed walks `v → v`.

**The $n = 1$ collider case is the strict bidirected reading, overriding the source's inline remark.** The relaxed "any adjacency" reading is rejected per the operator addition: the symbolic pattern collapses for $n = 1$ to require arrowheads at both endpoints, forcing the lone edge into `L`. The pattern match admits only `.bidir _`, rejecting both `.forwardE _` and `.backwardE _`.

**The $n = k = 1$ bifurcation case admits only a bidirected hinge.** Per the operator addition, both endnodes must have exactly one arrowhead pointing toward them. A directed hinge `.backwardE _` at $n = 1$ would encode $v_n \to v_0$, placing the arrowhead at $v_0$ and leaving $v_n$ with no arrowhead — explicitly excluded. The `IsBifurcationWithSplit` helper's length-1-tail branch admits only `.bidir _`; the directed-hinge specialisation rejects all three constructor tags on length-1 walks.

**`IsBifurcation`'s vertex-uniqueness clauses use `tail` and `dropLast` rather than full `Nodup`.** The LN constrains only the end-nodes, not interior vertices, which may repeat across the two arms of the bifurcation. `u ∉ p.vertices.tail` says `u` does not reappear after position 0; `v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast` says `v` does not appear before the last position. Full `Nodup` would be a strict over-restriction, and `u ≠ v` is kept as its own conjunct because the LN states it explicitly and it independently rules out non-trivial cycles `Walk G v v`.
